questions.question: Article 40.1 of the Constitution states that all citizens shall, as human persons, be held equal before the law. This does not mean that legislation cannot differentiate between classes of persons. What does the Article actually prohibit?

options.A: Any distinction between citizens based on socio-economic status
options.B: Differentiation based on any protected characteristic, including wealth
options.C: Arbitrary or irrational classification of persons for the purposes of legislation — differentiation must be based on relevant and reasonable distinctions
options.D: All distinctions between citizens in criminal legislation

questions.explanation: Article 40.1 does not demand identical treatment of all citizens. In Quinn's Supermarket v AG [1972] IR 1, the Supreme Court confirmed that Art. 40.1 permits legislative differentiation provided it is based on relevant and reasonable distinctions — not arbitrary or irrational. The Legislature may lawfully treat different categories of persons differently as long as the distinction is justified by rational differences in their capacity, social function or circumstances.

questions.question: In Quinn's Supermarket Ltd v AG [1972] IR 1, the Supreme Court upheld regulations that allowed Jewish traders to remain open on Sundays (which were closed days for others) to observe the Jewish Sabbath on Saturday. The constitutional significance of this ruling is:

options.A: That Article 40.1 was held inapplicable to commercial entities such as supermarkets
options.B: That differential treatment of religious groups for observance purposes is a rational and constitutionally permissible distinction under Art. 40.1
options.C: That freedom of religion under Art. 44 overrides equality under Art. 40.1 in all cases involving religion
options.D: That commercial equality claims cannot be heard by the Supreme Court without prior High Court determination

questions.explanation: Quinn's Supermarket upheld the special Sunday trading exemption for Jewish traders on the basis that Art. 40.1 permits differentiation where there is a rational basis for the distinction. Permitting observance of a different Sabbath day was a rational, relevant, and non-arbitrary distinction. The case also confirmed that 'equality before the law' is concerned with the human person's equality of dignity and legal standing, not absolute formal uniformity.

questions.question: The phrase in Art. 40.1 that the State may 'have due regard to differences of capacity, physical and moral, and of social function' has been used to justify which of the following legislative distinctions?

options.A: Differential retirement ages for men and women in the public service
options.B: Higher tax rates for corporations versus individuals
options.C: Different criminal sentencing regimes for adults vs juveniles
options.D: All of the above, where the distinction is grounded in genuine differences of capacity or social function

questions.explanation: The 'due regard to differences of capacity, physical and moral, and of social function' clause in Art. 40.1 provides the constitutional foundation for many legislative distinctions that would otherwise appear discriminatory. This clause was used (controversially) to justify age and sex distinctions in employment, and more generally supports differentiated treatment of adults and juveniles in criminal law. The key is that the distinction must genuinely reflect the relevant difference of capacity or function, not be a pretext for arbitrary discrimination.

questions.question: In de Burca v AG [1976] IR 38, the Supreme Court struck down jury selection provisions that systematically excluded women. Which constitutional provision was primarily engaged?

options.A: Article 41.2 — the provisions were consistent with the State's recognition of women's role in the home
options.B: Article 40.1 — the provisions were an unjustified and arbitrary discrimination against women as citizens
options.C: Article 38 — jury selection was not a fair procedure within the meaning of Art. 38.5
options.D: Article 34 — the provisions violated the right to a fair trial

questions.explanation: In de Burca v AG [1976] IR 38, the Supreme Court struck down provisions of the Juries Act 1927 that effectively excluded women from jury service (they had to opt in) as an unjustified discrimination against women as human persons in breach of Art. 40.1. The court rejected the argument that the exclusion was justified by the 'social function' of women in the home (Art. 41.2). This was a significant early ruling on gender equality in Irish constitutional law.

questions.question: Does Article 40.1 apply to distinctions made between citizens and non-citizens by the State?

options.A: Yes — Article 40.1 applies to all persons physically present in Ireland, including non-citizens
options.B: No — Article 40.1 expressly protects 'citizens' and the State may treat non-citizens differently; however, fundamental human rights under Art. 40.3 may protect non-citizens
options.C: No — the equal protection clause is exclusively concerned with commercial and taxation distinctions
options.D: Yes — the 1998 Good Friday Agreement extended Art. 40.1 rights to all persons on the island of Ireland

questions.explanation: Article 40.1 refers expressly to 'citizens'. The State may therefore treat non-citizens differently from citizens without necessarily breaching Art. 40.1 — e.g., restrictions on non-citizen access to State employment or voting. However, non-citizens may still be protected under other constitutional provisions: Art. 40.3 unenumerated rights and the right to bodily integrity apply to 'all persons', and fundamental human rights protections are not restricted to citizens alone (see Fajujonu v Minister for Justice [1990] 2 IR 151).

questions.question: A law imposes criminal penalties on street traders who operate without a licence, but expressly exempts 'traditional markets' from the licensing requirement. A street trader challenges the exemption as an unconstitutional inequality. The best analysis under Art. 40.1 is:

options.A: The exemption is automatically invalid as it treats two classes of trader differently
options.B: The exemption is valid if the distinction between traditional markets and street traders is rationally related to a legitimate objective, such as heritage preservation
options.C: The exemption violates Art. 40.1 because commercial distinctions are never permitted under the Constitution
options.D: The exemption is valid only if it was approved by referendum as a constitutional exception to equality

questions.explanation: Under Art. 40.1, legislative distinctions are permissible if they are based on rational and relevant differences between the groups. The distinction between 'traditional markets' and other street traders may be rationally based on heritage, planning, or community considerations — legitimate objectives. The court applies a rational basis review (not strict scrutiny) to most economic distinctions. The exemption would fail only if it were arbitrary, capricious or not genuinely connected to a legitimate objective.

questions.question: In Brennan v AG [1983] ILRM 449, the Supreme Court considered the constitutionality of differential land compensation for compulsory acquisition. The court emphasised that Art. 40.1 requires:

options.A: Identical compensation for all landowners regardless of location or use
options.B: That legislative distinctions in compensation schemes be grounded in genuine and relevant differences in the situations being regulated, not in arbitrary criteria
options.C: That compulsory acquisition compensation always equal market value, as any discount breaches equality
options.D: That compensation legislation be approved by the Seanad before taking effect

questions.explanation: Brennan v AG [1983] confirms that Art. 40.1 requires legislative classifications (including compensation schemes) to be based on genuine and relevant distinctions, not arbitrary criteria. Different compensation rates for different categories of land may be constitutionally justified if the categories are rationally differentiated. The court will not require identical treatment but will strike down classifications that are manifestly irrational or based on grounds having no connection to the legislative objective.

questions.question: Article 40.1 and the Employment Equality Acts 1998–2015 address equality in employment. What is the relationship between the constitutional guarantee and the statutory protection?

options.A: The statutory protection replaces Art. 40.1; constitutional equality in employment may no longer be litigated
options.B: Art. 40.1 is the constitutional floor; the equality legislation provides broader, more detailed protection going beyond the constitutional minimum in the employment sphere
options.C: Art. 40.1 provides greater protection than the legislation and overrides it in all cases of conflict
options.D: The legislation is invalid as it discriminates between protected and unprotected classes in breach of Art. 40.1

questions.explanation: Art. 40.1 provides the constitutional baseline for equality. The Employment Equality Acts 1998–2015 (implementing EU Directives) provide more detailed and expansive statutory protection across nine specific grounds (gender, age, race, religion, disability, sexual orientation, marital status, family status, and membership of the Traveller community). Where the statutory protection is more generous than the constitutional minimum, the legislation supplements rather than displaces Art. 40.1.

questions.question: Following the 34th Amendment in 2015 (marriage equality), which also inserted Art. 41.4, does Art. 40.1 now provide a free-standing constitutional basis for same-sex marriage rights independent of Art. 41.4?

options.A: Yes — Art. 40.1 always required equal access to marriage; Art. 41.4 merely confirms this
options.B: No — before the 34th Amendment, the Supreme Court in Zappone v Revenue Commissioners [2006] 2 IR 417 indicated that Art. 40.1 alone did not require recognition of same-sex marriages
options.C: Yes — the Supreme Court in Norris v AG [1984] IR 36 held that Art. 40.1 required equal treatment of gay relationships
options.D: No — Art. 41.4 is the exclusive constitutional basis for all marriage rights and Art. 40.1 has no independent role in that sphere

questions.explanation: Before the 34th Amendment 2015, the High Court in Zappone v Revenue Commissioners [2006] 2 IR 417 (McKechnie J) held that Art. 40.1, while guaranteeing equality as human persons, did not on the law as it stood require recognition of a same-sex marriage contracted abroad. The 34th Amendment resolved the constitutional position by inserting Art. 41.4 ('marriage may be contracted in accordance with law by two persons without distinction as to their sex'). Art. 40.1 alone was not treated pre-amendment as a sufficient basis to compel recognition.

questions.question: A statute provides for automatic suspension of driving licences upon conviction of any offence involving a vehicle, regardless of the circumstances of the offence. A person convicted of a minor parking offence challenges the mandatory suspension as a breach of Art. 40.1. What is the most legally sound argument in their favour?

options.A: All mandatory penalties are unconstitutional because they remove judicial discretion
options.B: The mandatory penalty treats all vehicle offenders identically regardless of the gravity of their offence, constituting an irrational failure to distinguish relevantly different situations, potentially breaching Art. 40.1
options.C: Art. 40.1 prohibits all criminal penalties for traffic offences as they relate to commercial activity
options.D: The suspension breaches Art. 40.3 property rights in the licence rather than Art. 40.1

questions.explanation: The argument that a mandatory, blanket penalty applied identically regardless of the gravity of the offence may violate Art. 40.1 rests on the principle that equality also requires differentiating between relevantly different situations. A person convicted of a serious dangerous driving offence is in a materially different situation from one convicted of a minor parking infringement. Treating them identically may be as irrational as treating relevantly similar situations differently. Irish courts have considered proportionality in mandatory sentencing in light of constitutional equality.
